summary:
Josh is an 18-19 year old first-year medicine student living in university halls with four flatmates, operating on an impressively tight food budget of £15-20 per week total. He presents as a thoughtful, budget-conscious young man who has developed sophisticated money-saving strategies around food - from hunting reduced sections at Morrison's to price-comparing delivery apps down to 50p differences. His relationship with food is notably mature for his age: he genuinely enjoys cooking as a creative hobby and experiments with recipes he finds online, viewing it as an affordable way to explore and create something meaningful for himself and his family when home.

Despite his careful budgeting, Josh has established a clear weekly ritual of treating himself to takeaway every Friday, which he frames as a reward for eating healthily during the week and 'something to work towards.' This suggests food delivery serves an important emotional function beyond mere convenience - it's his weekly moment of indulgence and choice freedom. His delivery behaviour is highly price-driven and methodical: he consistently compares between Just Eat and Deliveroo, preferring Just Eat for its cheaper prices and more independent restaurant options over chain fast food. The contradiction between his profile data (monthly ordering, no platforms used) and his actual weekly Friday routine suggests either social desirability bias in initial screening or genuine uncertainty about how to categorise his consistent but limited usage pattern.
personality_notes: Articulate but modest communicator who tends to downplay his achievements and routines as 'boring.' Shows analytical thinking in his systematic approach to food decisions and price comparison. Appears genuine and unpretentious, with occasional scattered responses suggesting he's thinking through answers in real-time rather than giving rehearsed responses.
food_relationship: Views cooking as an enjoyable creative hobby and uses delivery strategically as a weekly Friday treat within a highly disciplined £15-20 weekly food budget.

key_moments.description: Repeatedly emphasizes extreme price sensitivity, comparing apps down to 50p differences
key_moments.significance: Reveals the depth of his budget constraints and how this drives all food decisions

key_moments.description: Describes last takeaway order triggered by mother offering £10, leading to biryani and chocolate cake choice
key_moments.significance: Shows family financial support enables his treats and reveals his decision-making process when budget constraints are lifted

key_moments.description: Explains why he doesn't use grocery delivery despite attractive offers - spending over £10 creates too much food and waste
key_moments.significance: Demonstrates sophisticated understanding of his consumption patterns and storage limitations

what_not_said: Social eating or group orders with flatmates despite living with four others
Any mention of financial stress or hardship despite extreme budget consciousness
Discussion of nutritional concerns or health considerations beyond 'eating healthily'
Details about his medicine studies' impact on eating patterns beyond time constraints
initial_hypotheses: His Friday takeaway ritual serves as crucial emotional regulation and reward system within strict budgeting
Price sensitivity may be driven by family financial constraints rather than just student budget consciousness
His cooking hobby provides both creative outlet and practical cost-saving strategy
The discrepancy between profile and actual usage suggests complexity in how students categorize their delivery behavior
questions_for_coding: How do students balance emotional needs (treats, rewards) with financial constraints?
What role does family financial support play in delivery ordering decisions?
How do students define 'occasional' vs 'regular' usage when it comes to delivery apps?
What drives the choice between cooking as hobby vs convenience when time-pressed?
